title: The Architecture of Productivity
subtitle: Task management, focus, and career growth
description: Productivity is not the ability to do more in less time, but the ability to do the right things and see them through to results. This checklist combines the world's best practices for managing work, attention, and career.

items.title: Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)
items.text: 20% of efforts yield 80% of results. But this doesn't mean the remaining 80% of effort should be thrown away. The professional's task is to identify that 20% and direct more resources to them, and delegate, automate, or eliminate the rest.
items.tip: At the end of the week, write down all tasks and circle the ones that actually moved you toward your goals.

items.title: Eat That Frog
items.text: The most unpleasant and important task of the day should be done first. Procrastination eats mental energy all day. By completing the difficult task in the morning, you get a dopamine boost and free up your mind for the rest.
items.tip: In the evening, identify the 'frog' for tomorrow and don't check email until you've done it.

items.title: The Three-Email Rule
items.text: Constantly checking email and messengers is a system of interruptions imposed by others. Process incoming streams at strictly scheduled times (2-3 times a day), not reactively. Emails are someone else's priority list.
items.tip: Set an auto-reply: 'I check email at 11:00 and 16:00. Urgent — call me.'

items.title: The Pomodoro Technique
items.text: The brain cannot sustain peak concentration for hours. Working in intervals (25 minutes focus / 5 minutes rest) uses natural attention rhythms. This reduces resistance before starting a big task.
items.tip: For complex creative work, increase intervals to 52 minutes work / 17 minutes rest.

items.title: The Eisenhower Matrix
items.text: Everything has two dimensions: urgency and importance. People tend to do urgent but unimportant tasks (calls, notifications). True productivity lies in the 'Important but not urgent' quadrant: learning, strategy, health.
items.tip: Each morning, distribute tasks among 4 quadrants. Delegate or delete 'Not important and not urgent'.

items.title: The Power of NO
items.text: Focus is not about what you say 'yes' to. Focus is about the hundreds of things you say 'no' to. Each new commitment dilutes resources and reduces the quality of execution on key projects.
items.tip: Before agreeing, ask: 'What current task will I give up for this?'

items.title: Deep Work
items.text: In the knowledge economy, value is created only in a state of deep concentration. Shallow work (email, calls) is necessary but does not create a competitive advantage. Protect deep work blocks like a shrine.
items.tip: Put 2-3 hours of 'Meeting with Myself' on your calendar and don't let anyone move them.

items.title: Career Capital
items.source: Cal Newport — So Good They Can't Ignore You
items.text: Passion does not precede mastery; it follows it. Instead of asking 'What can the world give me?', ask 'What rare and valuable problem can I solve?' Rare skills give you leverage to negotiate for freedom and money.
items.tip: Identify one skill that is a 'bottleneck' in your industry and become the best at it.

items.title: Weekly Review
items.text: Without regular reflection, a planning system falls apart. The weekly review is when you clear your 'working memory', close loops, and build strategy for the next week.
items.tip: On Friday afternoon or Sunday evening, set aside 45 minutes to process inputs and plan.

items.title: Manage Energy, Not Time
items.source: Jim Loehr — The Power of Full Engagement
items.text: Time is a finite and fixed resource. Energy is renewable. Working 12 hours at low energy is less effective than 4 hours at peak. It's important to manage cycles of stress and recovery.
items.tip: Schedule complex tasks for your energy peak (for most, morning), and routine tasks for the slump.

quiz.q: What does the Pareto Principle state in the context of productivity?
What does Cal Newport call 'Deep Work'?
a: Activity in a state of maximum concentration

quiz.q: What is the essence of the Eisenhower Matrix?
a: Dividing tasks into urgent and important

quiz.q: According to Cal Newport, why is following passion a bad career strategy?
a: Mastery creates passion, not the other way around

quiz.q: What is more important during a workday: managing time or energy?
